meta_title: For Students: SaaS pricing to Solve can't finish projects (Advanced Case Study)
meta_description: Learn how to SaaS pricing and overcome can't finish projects. A Advanced-level Case Study using Antigravity and Claude Code.

content.seo_title: For Students: SaaS Pricing to Solve Project Abandonment
content.seo_description: Stop abandoning projects. Learn how to implement SaaS pricing to maintain focus and ship faster using Antigravity and Claude Code in this advanced case study.
content.answer_block: Project abandonment stems from scope creep and lack of market validation. By implementing a concrete SaaS pricing model early, you force yourself to define a specific value proposition for a paying user. This creates the necessary constraints to finish your build, prioritize features, and ship a high-value MVP efficiently.
content.intro:
You have a graveyard of half-finished GitHub repositories. It begins with a spark of genius, a weekend of frantic coding, and then the inevitable silence as you lose interest or get overwhelmed by feature bloat. You are not failing because you lack skill; you are failing because you lack constraints.

Professional developers survive by building for a price point, not for perfection. When you attach a dollar sign to your work, the 'nice-to-have' features vanish, replaced by the brutal necessity of delivering user value. This guide shows you how to use advanced AI tooling to bridge the gap between hobbyist tinkering and professional product delivery.

steps.title: Define the Value-Based Pricing Tiers
steps.description: Before writing a single line of code, document your pricing model. This dictates your feature set. We focus on a tiered structure that forces you to build the core engine first.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Analyze my project idea and propose three pricing tiers (Free, Pro, Enterprise) based on value-based metrics. Ensure the Pro tier contains only the essential features required for a functional MVP.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run 'generate a pricing strategy document based on my current README.md'

steps.title: Architecting the Payment Gateway Hook
steps.description: Integrate Stripe or LemonSqueezy early. By coding the checkout logic first, you anchor the entire application to the concept of a transaction.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Generate a secure backend service structure for Stripe subscriptions using Node.js, focusing on webhook handling for successful checkout events.

steps.title: Enforcing Feature Gating
steps.description: Implement middleware that restricts routes based on subscription status. This prevents scope creep by ensuring you only build what users are paying for.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Create an Express.js middleware that checks user subscription status in the database and blocks access to 'Pro' routes.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run 'implement subscription-based route protection in server.ts'

steps.title: Automating Usage Limits
steps.description: Use AI to write logic that monitors usage. This keeps your project scope tight and prevents you from building infinite features.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Write a service to track user API usage against their pricing tier and trigger an upgrade modal when they hit 80% capacity.

steps.title: Final Deployment and Validation
steps.description: Deploy to production to finalize the feedback loop. Shipping a paid product, even with limited features, is the ultimate cure for abandonment.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Create a deployment checklist for a SaaS application including database migrations, environment variable configuration, and monitoring setup.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run 'deploy to production and verify environment health'

pro_tips: Never build a feature unless it fits into one of your defined pricing tiers.
Use 'Time-Boxing' with Claude Code to force feature completion within 4-hour sprints.
Prioritize 'Stripe Test Mode' to ensure the payment flow is working before styling the UI.
The 'Enterprise' tier should only exist as a placeholder to anchor your Pro price point high.
If a feature takes longer than a weekend, it belongs in a V2 roadmap, not your MVP.
content.next_steps: Now that your pricing architecture is in place, go to your repository, delete any files that don't serve your core MVP, and commit to shipping the remaining features by the end of the week.
content.conversion_block: Stop building for yourself and start building for the market. By treating your code as a product, you gain the focus required to cross the finish line. Ready to build? Join our AI Builder community → Start your first project today.
